Shift work: No  0  0
Name of Hypothesis  Definition
Hapten hypothesis  Reactive metabolites are generated from drugs that can bind to endogenous proteins and form neoantigens, activating the immune system.
Pharmacological Interaction (p-i) Hypothesis  Certain drugs can act like small molecules and directly form non-covalent interactions with MHC molecules altering their binding pocket.
The altered peptide repertoire hypothesis  Drugs induce mistargeting of endogenous peptides to the wrong HLA leading to autoimmunity.
Multiple determinant hypothesis  Multiple risk factors (i.e., polymorphisms, age, gender), are necessary and overlap together to induce DILI.
Inflammatory stress hypothesis  A small inflammation occurring during drug therapy could interact with the action of the drug and escalate into liver injury.
The Concentration Ratio of p53 and DNA  p53 Molar Concentration (nmol/L)  The Quantity of p53 Adhering to DNA(/μm DNA)  Probability of DNA Binding Site Occupied by p53
The relationship between cortisol levels and the binding dynamics of p53 to DNA is a critical area of research, particularly in
the context of stress-related biological responses. The data table presented elucidates the cortisol concentrations at various time
intervals post-waking, specifically highlighting the values at waking, +4 hours, +8 hours, and +16 hours. Understanding these
cortisol fluctuations is essential, as they may influence the molecular mechanisms governing p53 activity. The table further
details the p53 molar concentration, measured in nmol/L, alongside metrics such as the concentration ratio of p53 to DNA and
the quantity of p53 adhering to DNA per micrometer of DNA. This information is pivotal for assessing the hypothesis that
reactive metabolites generated from drugs can bind to endogenous proteins, thereby forming neoantigens that activate the
immune system. By integrating the cortisol data with p53-binding metrics, this research aims to provide insights into the
physiological impacts of stress and its potential implications for immune responses and disease outcomes.
